checklist.You must be able to…: Identify + state functions of cell structures (cell wall, membrane, cytoplasm, nucleus, vacuoles, chloroplasts)
Identify + state functions of ER, Golgi body, mitochondria, ribosomes (from EM)
Compare typical animal vs plant cells
Explain how specialised cells are adapted (muscle, root hair, red blood cell)
Define diffusion; its role in nutrient uptake & gaseous exchange
Define osmosis; investigate + describe effects on plant & animal tissues
Define active transport; its importance (ion uptake by root hairs, glucose uptake in villi)
List elements in carbohydrates, fats, proteins
State main roles of carbohydrates, fats, proteins
Describe + carry out food tests: starch (iodine), reducing sugar (Benedict's), protein (biuret), fats (ethanol)
State large molecules built from basic units (starch/glycogen←glucose; protein←amino acids; fat←glycerol+fatty acids)
Explain enzyme action: active site, enzyme-substrate complex, lowers activation energy, specificity, lock-and-key
Investigate + explain effect of temperature & pH on enzyme rate
Functions of digestive-system parts (mouth→anus): ingestion, digestion, absorption, assimilation, egestion
Describe peristalsis
Functions of enzymes in digestion (amylase, maltase, protease, lipase) + substrates/products
Explain how villus structure (capillaries, lacteal) suits absorption
State role of hepatic portal vein
Role of liver (glucose↔glycogen, fat digestion, amino-acid metabolism→urea, breakdown of alcohol & hormones)
Outline effects of alcohol on brain + long-term effects + social implications
Identify main blood vessels to/from heart, lungs, liver, kidney
Relate structure of arteries, veins, capillaries to function
Describe transfer of materials between capillaries & tissue fluid
Components of blood + roles (RBC/haemoglobin, plasma, WBC, platelets/clotting)
ABO blood groups + donor/recipient combinations
Structure & function of heart (muscular contraction, valves)
Outline cardiac cycle (systole/diastole)
Coronary heart disease: causes, prevention
Identify larynx→alveoli + functions in gaseous exchange
Describe breathing (cilia, diaphragm, ribs, intercostal muscles)
Explain how alveolus structure suits gaseous exchange
Toxic components of tobacco smoke (nicotine, tar, CO) + effects
Define aerobic respiration + word & symbol equation
Define anaerobic respiration in humans + word equation
Explain oxygen debt during vigorous exercise
Define excretion + importance of removing nitrogenous compounds
Identify kidneys, ureter, bladder, urethra + functions
Outline nephron function: ultra-filtration & selective reabsorption
Outline mechanism of dialysis in kidney failure
Define homeostasis
Explain principles: stimulus, corrective mechanism, negative feedback
Maintenance of body temperature (receptors, sweating, shivering, blood flow, hypothalamus)
Define a hormone
Endocrine gland (islets of Langerhans)
Explain regulation of blood glucose by insulin & glucagon
Describe type-2 diabetes
Risk factors + management of type-2 diabetes
Outline role of ADH in osmoregulation
State role of nervous system (brain, spinal cord, nerves)
Outline reflex action (receptor→sensory→relay→motor→effector)
Describe structure of the eye (front & horizontal section)
State functions of eye parts (focused image on retina)
Describe pupil reflex to bright/dim light
Infectious vs non-infectious diseases + examples
Pathogens (bacteria, viruses); spread via body fluids, food, water
Virus structure (protein coat + genetic material; reproduces in host)
Bacteria structure (cell wall, DNA no nucleus; pathogenic/non-)
Signs/symptoms of influenza & pneumococcal disease
Transmission + reduction for influenza & pneumococcus
How vaccines work (stimulate WBC to produce antibodies)
How antibiotics target bacteria but not viruses
Discuss antibiotic misuse → resistance
Cellular/tissue structure of a dicot leaf (transverse section) + significance
Positions & functions of xylem & phloem in leaf & stem
Explain how root hair cell suits water & ion uptake
Chlorophyll absorbs light → chemical energy for carbohydrates
Why most life depends on photosynthesis
State photosynthesis equation (words & symbols)
Describe how CO2 reaches mesophyll cells
Investigate effect of light, CO2, temperature on photosynthesis rate
Discuss limiting factors
Define transpiration (consequence of gaseous exchange)
Explain water movement between cells & environment via water potential
Outline water pathway root→xylem→leaf by transpiration pull
Investigate effect of air movement, temp, humidity, light on transpiration; how wilting occurs
Define translocation (sucrose in phloem)
Describe non-cyclical nature of energy flow
Roles of producers, consumers, decomposers in food chains/webs
Explain energy losses along food chains; efficiency between trophic levels
Describe + interpret pyramids of numbers & biomass
Carbon cycle; forests & oceans as carbon sinks
How human activity raises atmospheric CO2 → global warming
Discuss actions to reduce global warming
Effects of pollution (sewage, plastics, insecticides/biomagnification)
Discuss conservation & sustainable use → biodiversity
Outline relationships among DNA, genes, chromosomes
DNA = double helix, two strands of nucleotides (sugar+phosphate+base)
State complementary base pairing (A-T, C-G)
Each gene: sequence of nucleotides, codes for one polypeptide, unit of inheritance
DNA carries genetic code to synthesise polypeptides
Genes transferred between organisms → transgenic organisms
How insulin gene inserted into bacterial DNA (biotechnology)
Discuss benefits & ethics of genetic engineering
Define asexual reproduction
State mitosis (genetically identical, chromosome number maintained)
Importance of mitosis (growth, repair, asexual reproduction)
Define sexual reproduction
Define haploid/diploid; need for reduction division
Homologous pairs of chromosomes
State meiosis (genetically dissimilar, number halved)
Meiosis in gamete formation
Flower structure, pollination (self/cross), insect vs wind, pollen tube → fertilisation
Male reproductive system + functions
Female reproductive system + functions
Outline menstrual cycle (progesterone & oestrogen; fertile/infertile phases)
Fertilisation & early development (implantation)
Functions of amniotic sac & fluid
Function of placenta & umbilical cord (exchange)
Transmission of HIV + reduction
Distinguish gene vs allele
Explain dominant, recessive, codominant, homozygous, heterozygous, phenotype, genotype
Predict simple crosses (3:1 and 1:1 ratios)
Explain why observed ratios differ from expected
Use genetic diagrams to solve monohybrid problems
Codominance & multiple alleles (ABO blood groups)
Sex determination (XX / XY)
Mutation (sickle cell; Down syndrome)
Ionising radiation & chemical mutagens increase mutation rate
Continuous vs discontinuous variation + examples
Variation & competition → differential survival
Environmental factors as forces of natural selection
Explain natural selection as mechanism for evolution

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Net movement of particles from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration, down a concentration gradient.
chains.Common trap: Must say NET movement + 'down a concentration gradient'.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Net movement of water molecules from a region of higher water potential to a region of lower water potential, through a partially permeable membrane, down a water potential gradient.
chains.Common trap: 'Partially' (not 'semi') permeable; it's water molecules, not 'water'.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Water enters the cell by osmosis → vacuole expands → pushes cytoplasm against the cell wall → cell becomes turgid; the cell wall prevents bursting.
chains.Common trap: Turgid, not 'swollen'. Wall stops it bursting.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Water leaves by osmosis → cytoplasm & membrane shrink and pull away from the cell wall → cell is plasmolysed.
chains.Common trap: Plasmolysis = membrane pulls from wall.

chains.Concept: Red blood cell in dilute solution
chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Water enters by osmosis → cell swells and bursts (haemolysis) because there is no cell wall to resist expansion.
chains.Common trap: Contrast with plant cell — no wall.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Movement of particles from a region of lower concentration to a region of higher concentration, against a concentration gradient, using energy released from respiration.
chains.Common trap: Must include 'against gradient' AND 'energy from respiration'.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Substrate binds to the active site of the enzyme forming an enzyme-substrate complex; the active site is complementary in shape to the substrate (specificity); the enzyme lowers the activation energy.
chains.Common trap: Complementary shape; 'lock and key'; lowers activation energy.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: As temperature rises, rate increases (molecules gain kinetic energy → more frequent effective collisions) up to the optimum; above the optimum the enzyme is denatured — its active site changes shape so substrate no longer fits, and rate falls.
chains.Common trap: 'Denatured' NOT 'killed'. Denaturation is permanent.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Each enzyme works fastest at its optimum pH; at extreme pH the enzyme is denatured — active site changes shape — so rate falls.
chains.Common trap: Name the change: active site changes shape.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Villi and microvilli give a large surface area; the epithelium is one cell thick giving a short diffusion distance; a dense capillary network maintains a steep concentration gradient; a lacteal absorbs fatty acids & glycerol.
chains.Common trap: 4 marks = 4 features. Lacteal for fats.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Artery: thick, muscular, elastic wall to withstand high pressure, narrow lumen. Vein: thinner wall, wider lumen, valves to prevent backflow. Capillary: wall one cell thick → short diffusion distance for exchange.
chains.Common trap: Match each structure to its pressure/exchange function.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Biconcave disc → large surface area to volume ratio for oxygen diffusion; no nucleus → more room for haemoglobin; haemoglobin binds oxygen to form oxyhaemoglobin.
chains.Common trap: No nucleus = more haemoglobin, not 'flexible'.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water (+ energy released).  C6H12O6 + 6O2 → 6CO2 + 6H2O.
chains.Common trap: Energy is 'released', not 'produced/made'.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Glucose → lactic acid (+ a small amount of energy released).
chains.Common trap: In humans it's lactic acid (yeast = ethanol + CO2).

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Large surface area, wall one cell thick (short diffusion distance), moist surface, and a good blood supply that maintains a steep concentration gradient for gaseous exchange.
chains.Common trap: 4 marks = 4 features.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: During vigorous exercise oxygen supply is insufficient, so muscles respire anaerobically producing lactic acid; the oxygen debt is repaid by continued rapid, deep breathing which oxidises the lactic acid.
chains.Common trap: Lactic acid must be oxidised/removed.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: High blood pressure in the glomerulus forces small molecules (water, glucose, salts, urea) out of the blood into the Bowman's capsule; large molecules (blood cells, proteins) remain in the blood.
chains.Common trap: High pressure + size selectivity.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Useful substances are reabsorbed into the blood: all glucose, some water and some salts; urea and excess water remain to form urine.
chains.Common trap: 'ALL glucose' reabsorbed (in a healthy person).

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: A change in the internal environment is detected; a corrective mechanism is triggered that reverses the change and returns the condition towards its set point.
chains.Common trap: Corrective mechanism REVERSES the change.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Temperature receptors in the skin detect the rise → hypothalamus coordinates → vasodilation (more blood flows near skin surface, more heat lost) and increased sweating (evaporation of sweat removes heat).
chains.Common trap: Vasodilation ≠ vessels move. More blood flow near surface.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Islets of Langerhans in the pancreas secrete insulin → liver converts excess glucose to glycogen for storage → blood glucose falls back to normal.
chains.Common trap: Insulin from pancreas; storage as glycogen in liver.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Stimulus → receptor → sensory neurone → relay neurone (in spinal cord) → motor neurone → effector → response.
chains.Common trap: Fixed order; relay neurone is in the spinal cord/CNS.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Carbon dioxide + water → (light energy, chlorophyll) glucose + oxygen.  6CO2 + 6H2O → C6H12O6 + 6O2.
chains.Common trap: Light energy & chlorophyll go ABOVE the arrow.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: The factor in shortest supply that limits the rate of the process; increasing it raises the rate until another factor becomes limiting.
chains.Common trap: Explain the plateau on rate graphs with this.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Water evaporates from mesophyll cell surfaces and diffuses out through stomata; this lowers water potential in the leaf, drawing water up the xylem as a continuous column from the roots.
chains.Common trap: Water pulled UP xylem; continuous column.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: DNA is two strands of nucleotides; adenine pairs with thymine (A-T) and cytosine pairs with guanine (C-G).
chains.Common trap: A-T, C-G exactly.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: State parental phenotypes → genotypes → gametes (circle them) → Punnett square → offspring genotypes → offspring phenotype ratio.
chains.Common trap: Marks for gametes circled + full working, not just the answer.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: Alleles I^A and I^B are codominant, I^O is recessive; genotype I^A I^B gives blood group AB because both alleles are expressed.
chains.Common trap: Both alleles expressed = codominance.

chains.Mark-scheme chain — write THIS to score: There is variation in a population; a selection pressure (competition/predation/environment) means better-adapted individuals are more likely to survive and reproduce, passing on their favourable alleles; over many generations the favourable allele becomes more common.
chains.Common trap: Full chain: variation → pressure → differential survival → inheritance → allele frequency change.

diagrams.Must label: Cell wall, membrane, cytoplasm, nucleus, vacuole, chloroplast, mitochondria
diagrams.Skill / calculation: Compare table: what plant has that animal lacks

diagrams.Must label: Clean single lines, no shading, ruled label lines, title, magnification
diagrams.Skill / calculation: Magnification = image size ÷ actual size; keep units consistent

diagrams.Must label: Epithelium (one cell), capillary network, lacteal, microvilli
diagrams.Skill / calculation: Link each labelled part to absorption

diagrams.Must label: 4 chambers, atria/ventricles, valves, aorta, vena cava, pulmonary artery/vein
diagrams.Skill / calculation: Trace blood flow deoxygenated vs oxygenated side

diagrams.Must label: Alveolus, capillary, RBC, O2/CO2 diffusion arrows
diagrams.Skill / calculation: State the 4 adaptations for gas exchange

diagrams.Must label: Glomerulus, Bowman's capsule, tubule, collecting duct; cortex/medulla
diagrams.Skill / calculation: Say where ultrafiltration vs reabsorption occurs

diagrams.Must label: Receptor, sensory neurone, relay neurone (spinal cord), motor neurone, effector
Cornea, iris, pupil, lens, ciliary body, retina, optic nerve
Waxy cuticle, upper epidermis, palisade & spongy mesophyll, air spaces, xylem/phloem, stomata, guard cells
Root hair (extension), cytoplasm, vacuole, cell wall
diagrams.Skill / calculation: Explain large surface area for water/ion uptake

diagrams.Must label: Sepal, petal, stamen (anther, filament), carpel (stigma, style, ovary, ovule)
Testes, sperm duct, prostate, urethra, penis / ovary, oviduct, uterus, cervix, vagina
Two strands, sugar-phosphate backbone, base pairs A-T & C-G
Trophic levels, producers/consumers/decomposers; pyramid of numbers & biomass; CO2 in/out
diagrams.Skill / calculation: Explain energy loss between levels; interpret pyramids
Full working scores, not just the ratio
